coredata.dc:title: Microarray analysis of gene expression differences in microglia after exposure to graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Xue
Wu, Tianshu
Tang, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been broadly applied in biomedicine in recent years. So far, researches have reported that GQDs might contribute to the injury of the central nervous system (CNS), yet the latent toxicological mechanism is not clear. This study aims to investigate the underlying biological mechanism responsible for the neurotoxicity of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) by use of genome-wide transcription microarray. The findings showed that 174 and 1341 genes were altered significantly in the BV2 cells treated by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs compared with the control, respectively. As for the BV2 cells exposed to 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, 1396 diversely expressed genes were detected. By comparing the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs exposed group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs exposed group, the expression of 256 genes was extensively altered, including 58 upregulated genes and 198 downregulated genes. From Gene Ontology (GO) analysis, the altered genes were mainly enriched in functions of ion channel activation and cellular processes. Based on the KEGG pathway and signal-net analysis, the toxicity of GQDs in BV2 cells was closely related to calcium signaling pathway, cell cycle and endocytosis. And the pathways that the shared mRNAs involved all served as the crucial roles in the neurotoxicity of GQDs despite the chemical functionalization (N-GQDs or A-GQDs). In addition, the consequences from qRT-PCR, Western blot, intracellular calcium level measurements and comet assay further confirmed that calcium dyshomeostasis, DNA damage and cell cycle arrest were the key factors responsible for the GQDs-induced neurotoxicity through affecting several classical signaling pathways. In conclusion, our research will supply essential data for further studies on mechanisms of GQDs-induced neurotoxicity by use of genome-wide screening.

head.ce:title: Microarray analysis of gene expression differences in microglia after exposure to graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been broadly applied in biomedicine in recent years. So far, researches have reported that GQDs might contribute to the injury of the central nervous system (CNS), yet the latent toxicological mechanism is not clear. This study aims to investigate the underlying biological mechanism responsible for the neurotoxicity of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) by use of genome-wide transcription microarray. The findings showed that 174 and 1341 genes were altered significantly in the BV2 cells treated by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs compared with the control, respectively. As for the BV2 cells exposed to 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, 1396 diversely expressed genes were detected. By comparing the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs exposed group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs exposed group, the expression of 256 genes was extensively altered, including 58 upregulated genes and 198 downregulated genes. From Gene Ontology (GO) analysis, the altered genes were mainly enriched in functions of ion channel activation and cellular processes. Based on the KEGG pathway and signal-net analysis, the toxicity of GQDs in BV2 cells was closely related to calcium signaling pathway, cell cycle and endocytosis. And the pathways that the shared mRNAs involved all served as the crucial roles in the neurotoxicity of GQDs despite the chemical functionalization (N-GQDs or A-GQDs). In addition, the consequences from qRT-PCR, Western blot, intracellular calcium level measurements and comet assay further confirmed that calcium dyshomeostasis, DNA damage and cell cycle arrest were the key factors responsible for the GQDs-induced neurotoxicity through affecting several classical signaling pathways. In conclusion, our research will supply essential data for further studies on mechanisms of GQDs-induced neurotoxicity by use of genome-wide screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a novel material composed of monolayer carbon atom with a honeycomb-like structure. And the graphene-based materials have attracted significant attention all over the world since discovered in 2004 because of their superior physical-chemical characteristics as well as unique structure (Yuan et al., 2014; Feng et al., 2020; Novoselov et al., 2004). As is known to us all, the release of heavy metals have extensively restricted the biomedical applications of traditional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdTe), which usually consists of the elements from II-VI or III-V group (Liang et al., 2020). Then Silvestrov and Efetov defined a type of zero-dimensional material named as Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in 2007 (Silvestrov and Efetov, 2007). GQDs possess the same structure of monoatomic layer as graphene with their lateral dimensions less than 100 nm, thus combining the superiorities of graphene as well as quantum dots (QDs) (Li et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020). Therefore, GQDs have been considered to own broad application prospect in the field of therapeutics. Particularly, researchers have paid much attention to the biomedical application of GQDs, which mainly includes biological imaging, drug delivery, bacteriostatic action and so on (Tabish et al., 2018; Ko et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2012; Qi et al., 2013).

Considering the broad application prospect of GQDs in the biomedicine area, the bio-safety of GQDs has received abundant concern from researchers all over the world. A research group found that GQDs could obstruct autophagy flux by reducing the quantity and activity of cathepsin B as well as suppressing lysosome proteolysis ability in GC-2 and TM4 cells, which possibly led to potential male reproduction problems (Ji et al., 2016). Another group reported that hydroxylation of GQDs would result in G0-G1 retardation and cellular senescence (Tian et al., 2016). In addition, it was found that the up-regulation of caspase genes and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (e.g., OH) also made contributions to the DNA cleavage (Xu et al., 2018). However, the underlying neurotoxicity of graphene has been hardly researched to our best knowledge. As we all know, the brain shows hypersensitivity to inflammation, hypoxia as well as oxidative stress and the regenerative capacity of neurons is really limited, which makes the brain extremely vulnerable. Li et.al found that the graphene-based nanomaterials could do harm to the dopaminergic and glutamatergic neurons, and long-term exposure to graphene-based nanomaterials was likely to have neurotoxicity risks of inducing praxeology impairment and neural injury (Li et al., 2017). Furthermore, another group reported that the graphene oxide deposited by polyethylene glycol (PEG) could pass through the blood-brain barrier and significantly damage the nervous system (Mendonca et al., 2016). What's worse, GQDs can easily intrude into the central nervous system (CNS) due to their tiny size. Therefore, the toxicity of GQDs toward the CNS deserves much attention for the CNS is a main target of GQDs. On the other hand, it is necessary to carry out deep exploration of the potential toxic mechanism of GQDs toward the CNS to better restrain their toxicity in the future.

In this research, N-GQDs and A-GQDs were applied for their low toxicity, superior water stability, desirable biocompatibility as well as brilliant photoelectric property (Junkermeier et al., 2013). Furthermore, the BV2 microglial cell line is a resident cell and is usually considered as a biological model for the research of CNS, which was hence applied to our study (Wu et al., 2019). However, there still exists significant difference between the damage phenotype and precise molecular mechanism. And the appliance of emerging techniques in traditional toxicological research will contribute to the exploration toxic mechanisms. The main purpose of this study is to discover the latent pathways by which GQDs cause the toxicity of CNS based on the microarray genome-wide analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The acquisition of N-GQDs and A-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs and A-GQDs used in this study were purchased from XFNANO materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were acquired on an electron microscope. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out on a Malcern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument.

The BV2 microglial cell line was provided by Shanghai Cell Research Center. The Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) was applied in the cell culture. The 96-well plates were used to pre-incubate BV2 cells with 100 μL medium and 5 × 103 cells per well. Meanwhile, the BV2 cells were treated with different concentrations of GQDs (0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 250, 500 μg/mL). After incubation for 24 h, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (0.5 mg/mL, Sigma Aldrich, MO, USA) was introduced, followed by incubation for another 4 h in a humid atmosphere (37 °C). Afterwards, removing the culture medium and adding the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (Sigma Aldrich, MO, USA). Then a spectrophotometer was employed to record the absorbance of each well and the cell viability was calculated by the following formula: Cell viability% = Absorbance of (experimental group − blank control group) / absorbance of (control group − blank control group) × 100%.

In this section, the BV2 cells were cultured in 6-well plates, followed by treatment with DMEM containing different types or concentrations of GQDs (25 μg/mL N-GQDs, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs) after completely attached to the bottom area. Meanwhile, the BV2 cells treated with common DMEM were used as the control group. And three replicates were set for each group. After incubation for 10 min at room temperature in the dark, following the manufacturer's instructions (YEASEN, Shanghai, China), the apoptosis rate was recorded using a FACScan (FCM) (BD FACSCCanto™ II, USA) by collecting corresponding fluorescence signals of FITC and PI from the fluorescence FL1 and the FL2 channels, respectively.

In this experiment, the BV2 cells were fixed in PBS containing 2.5% glutaraldehyde, followed by the standard fixation protocol reported in a previous study (Wu et al., 2016). And the ultrathin sections of cells were observed by use of a TEM.

The quantification of total RNA was carried out using NanoDrop ND-2000 (Thermo Scientific). And the Agilent Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent Technologies) was employed to detect the RNA integrity, followed by sample labeling, microarray hybridization and washing based on the manufacturer's standard process. In brief, total RNA was reversely transcribed to DS-cDNA and then transcribed to cRNA, following labeled by use of Cyanine-3-CTP. Afterwards, the labeled cRNA was hybridized with the microarray and washed. Then the Agilent Scanner G2505C (Agilent Technologies) was used to scan the arrays to obtain the original image for subsequent analysis.

The array images were analyzed by use of Feature Extraction software (version10.7.1.1, Agilent Technologies) to obtain the initial data, which was then analyzed using Genespring (version 14.8, Agilent Technologies). Firstly, the initial data was normalized with the quantile algorithm. Then the normalized data was filtrated and the probes having flags in “Detected” at least 1 out of 2 conditions were selected for further analysis. And the fold change and P value was calculated by t-test to ascertain the differentially expressed genes. The fold change ≥2.0 and P value ≤0.05 was set as the threshold for up- and down-regulated genes. The roles of these differentially expressed mRNAs were subsequently identified with GO and KEGG analysis. Finally, the distinguishable genes' expression pattern among samples was exhibited by Hierarchical Clustering.

Furthermore, gene signal transduction network (Signal-net) was conducted to explore the genetic regulation network on basis of the differential gene expression and KEGG pathway consequences. In fact, the main purpose was to distinguish pivotal genes which acted as the intermedium of precise response to upstream and downstream genes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Equal quantities of total RNA from microglia were used to conduct the qRT-PCR analysis. The qRT-PCR primers were designed by software Primer Premier based on National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) (Supplementary Table 1). The relative quantities of mRNA were normalized against the internal reference gene gapdh. Three replicates were conducted for each qRT-PCR analysis.

Equal quantities of total RNA from microglia were used to conduct the qRT-PCR analysis. The qRT-PCR primers were designed by software Primer Premier based on National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) (Supplementary Table 1). The relative quantities of mRNA were normalized against the internal reference gene gapdh. Three replicates were conducted for each qRT-PCR analysis.

The total protein content of BV2 cells was extracted by RIPA with PMSF (100:1) (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Shanghai, China) following the manufacturer's instructions. The bicinchoninic acid (BCA) protein assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was applied to measure the protein concentrations of the extracts in each group. An equal amount (30–40 μg) of protein was separated on a 10% or 6% SDS-PAGE gel and electro-transferred to nitrocellulose membranes blocked with 5% skim milk in Tris-buffered saline (TBS) containing 0.05% Tween-20 (TBST), followed by rocking gently for at least 1 h at room temperature. Then the membranes were incubated with primary antibodies, i.e. anti-YAP1 (A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-p-YAP1 (AP0489, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-TAZ (A8202, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-p-TAZ (AP0919, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-p38 (A14401, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-p-p38 (AP1165, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-JNK (A4867, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), anti-p-JNK (AP0276, A1002, ABclonal, Wuhan, China), and anti-GAPDH (5174, Cell Signaling Technology, USA) at 1:1000 dilution overnight (4 °C). After washed three times with TBST, the membranes were incubated with HRP conjugated anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, Inc.) (1:10000 dilution) for 1 h at room temperature. Protein bands were visualized by use of the chemiluminescence reagent (Millipore, USA) and the films were analyzed by Image J software. The experiment was repeated independently at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comet assay and verification of intracellular calcium level
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the verifications of comet assay and intracellular calcium level was carried out to validate the microarray consequences on account of the possible false positive and technological error. The DNA damage of BV2 cells treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs were measured by the comet assay, which was carried out using the published method (He et al., 2019). In addition, the relevant indexes of 50 cells were accomplished for subsequent comparison, including tail DNA%, tail length and Olive tail moment.

As for the verification of intracellular calcium level, the BV2 cells in a 6-well plate were firstly treated with culture mediums appended nothing, BAPTA-AM (20 μM), 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs + BAPTA-AM (20 μM), 100 μg/mL A-GQDs + BAPTA-AM (20 μM), respectively. After treatment of 24 h, the cells were washed with Hanks balanced salt solution (HBSS) (Beyotime, China) for three times to wipe off the original culture medium. Subsequently, the BV2 cells were treated with fluo-4/AM (5 μM) (Yeasen, China), followed by maintained for 30 min in the dark. The BV2 cells were then rinsed by the same method above to wipe off the residual fluorescent dye. Prior to imaging, the BV2 cells were hatched for 20–25 min (37 °C), followed by fluorescence intensity measurement using a laser-scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) (Olympus, FV1000).

All data are displayed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) and statistical analysis was carried out using Graphpad prism 8. And one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was employed to determine the statistical significance between the control and exposed groups, followed by Dunnett's t-test to analyze the significance of differences among groups. P values of <0.05 were regarded as statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization data of N-GQDs and A-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and sizes of N-GQDs and A-GQDs used in this research were presented in Fig. 1A–F. From the TEM images (Fig. 1A and D), both individual N-GQDs and A-GQDs were uniform with an average particle size of approximately 3 nm and 4 nm, respectively. And the lattice structure of N-GQDs seemed better than that of A-GQDs. In order to accord with the cell culture environment, the sizes of N-GQDs and A-GQDs were assessed by dissolved in deionized (DI) water and DMEM at the concentration of 100 μg/mL. As for the hydrodynamic diameter, the mean sizes of N-GQDs in DI water (4.19 nm) and DMEM (3.45 nm) were slightly higher than that measured by TEM (Fig. 1B and C). And the mean sizes of A-GQDs in DI water and DMEM were both 5.55 nm (Fig. 1E and F), which were also higher than that measured by TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of GQDs on BV2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability consequences were illustrated in Fig. 2A–B. It could be found that the cells viability of BV2 cells showed a negligible decrease when exposed to 25 μg/mL GQDs. And the cell viability decreased evidently after BV2 cells were exposed to 100–500 μg/mL GQDs for 24 h. This indicated that the BV2 cells showed high sensibility to GQDs even in a relatively low dose of 100 μg/mL. As for the groups treated by N-GQDs (100 μg/mL) and A-GQDs (100 μg/mL), the cell viability decreased to 78.28 ± 0.03% and 82.51 ± 0.06%, respectively. Furthermore, the BV2 cells treated by N-GQDs (25 and 100 μg/mL) and A-GQDs (100 μg/mL) were selected for subsequent research to identify the responsive genes of BV2 cells exposed to low-concentration GQDs as well as the changed genes of the groups with approximately 80% cell viability.

To further determine the toxicity of N-GQDs and A-GQDs on BV2 cells, the apoptosis rates were measured. From Fig. 2C, it could be found that the apoptosis and necrosis rates of BV2 cells were elevated by N-GQDs and A-GQDs.

Furthermore, the intracellular structure of BV2 cells by TEM images were illustrated in Fig. 2D. It could be observed that the treatment of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs resulted in various impairments of mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) of BV2 cells, including broken mitochondrial ridge, collapse of mitochondrial membrane and ER dilation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene expression alteration induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The gene expression in the group exposed to 25 μg/mL N-GQDs was illustrated in Fig. 2E. It could be observed that 174 genes' expression was distinctly altered in contrast to the control group, including 74 upregulated genes and 100 downregulated genes. And the gene expression alteration in experimental groups of relatively high dose was more significant than that of 25 μg/mL treated group. As for the group treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, 1341 genes' expression was obviously altered, including 688 upregulated genes and 653 downregulated genes (Fig. 2F). And 1396 genes' expression was remarkably changed in the group exposed to 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, including 772 upregulated genes and 624 downregulated genes (Fig. 2G). By comparing the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs exposed group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs exposed group, it was found that 256 genes' expression was extensively altered, including 58 upregulated genes and 198 downregulated genes (Fig. 2H, Supplementary Table 2). Furthermore, Supplementary Tables 3–6 summarized the top 10 extensively changed genes by comparing different experimental groups, including 25 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS control group, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS control group, 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group VS control group and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group.

The gene expression in the group exposed to 25 μg/mL N-GQDs was illustrated in Fig. 2E. It could be observed that 174 genes' expression was distinctly altered in contrast to the control group, including 74 upregulated genes and 100 downregulated genes. And the gene expression alteration in experimental groups of relatively high dose was more significant than that of 25 μg/mL treated group. As for the group treated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, 1341 genes' expression was obviously altered, including 688 upregulated genes and 653 downregulated genes (Fig. 2F). And 1396 genes' expression was remarkably changed in the group exposed to 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, including 772 upregulated genes and 624 downregulated genes (Fig. 2G). By comparing the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs exposed group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs exposed group, it was found that 256 genes' expression was extensively altered, including 58 upregulated genes and 198 downregulated genes (Fig. 2H, Supplementary Table 2). Furthermore, Supplementary Tables 3–6 summarized the top 10 extensively changed genes by comparing different experimental groups, including 25 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS control group, 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS control group, 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group VS control group and 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group VS 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Predicted functions of differentially expressed genes induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GO annotation analysis was employed to achieve the gene function categories of BV2 cells treated by GQDs because the GO database supplies professional terminology to make clear the peculiarities of gene products, including Biological Process (BP), Cellular Component (CC) and Molecular Function (MF). Generally, BP has the strongest correlation with the experimental researches and represents the process of molecular activity events, including the functional collection of cells, tissues, organs, and species. CC often represents a cell or its external environment, while MF is an active element describing a gene product at the molecular level. By the analysis of gene set enrichment, it was easy to discern the significant functional items. Furthermore, the GO analysis results can be coupled with biological significance to obtain the genes for following research. The items owning lower P-value usually suggests the gathered GO items possibly related to the toxicity by GQDs. The GO map illustrated the significantly functional categories. In addition, the top 10 items of GO annotation were respectively presented in Supplementary Fig. 1A–H on basis of the upregulated and deregulated genes. Of note, a bigger –lgP score indicated a smaller P-value, which was calculated by -log10(P-value).

GO annotation analysis was employed to achieve the gene function categories of BV2 cells treated by GQDs because the GO database supplies professional terminology to make clear the peculiarities of gene products, including Biological Process (BP), Cellular Component (CC) and Molecular Function (MF). Generally, BP has the strongest correlation with the experimental researches and represents the process of molecular activity events, including the functional collection of cells, tissues, organs, and species. CC often represents a cell or its external environment, while MF is an active element describing a gene product at the molecular level. By the analysis of gene set enrichment, it was easy to discern the significant functional items. Furthermore, the GO analysis results can be coupled with biological significance to obtain the genes for following research. The items owning lower P-value usually suggests the gathered GO items possibly related to the toxicity by GQDs. The GO map illustrated the significantly functional categories. In addition, the top 10 items of GO annotation were respectively presented in Supplementary Fig. 1A–H on basis of the upregulated and deregulated genes. Of note, a bigger –lgP score indicated a smaller P-value, which was calculated by -log10(P-value).

The KEGG analysis was applied to confirm the changed biological pathways after exposure of GQDs, which would contribute to research the underlying neurotoxicity pathways by GQDs (Fig. 3A–H). In the group exposed to 25 μg/mL N-GQDs, there appeared 3 and 17 remarkable pathways corresponded respectively to upregulated and downregulated genes. As for the group treated by 100 μg/mL N-GQDs, 34 pathways were obviously recognized, including 9 pathways related to upregulated genes and 25 pathways relevant to downregulated genes. Meanwhile, 5 remarkable pathways were identified to be related to upregulated genes while 15 remarkable pathways corresponded to downregulated genes in 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment group. Comparing the group of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment with that treated by 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, 6 remarkable pathways were identified, including 2 pathways related to upregulated genes and 4 pathways related to downregulated genes.

In the group of 25 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment, the remarkable pathways ascertained by upregulated genes included caffeine metabolism, Epstein-Barr virus infection and one carbon pool by folate signaling pathway. And the downregulated genes in the group treated by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs significantly enriched in Wnt signaling pathway, pathway in cancer and cell cycle, as summarized in Supplementary Tables 7–8. The changed pathways in the group exposed to 100 μg/mL N-GQDs were listed in Supplementary Tables 9–10. It was found that the upregulated genes were mainly enriched in calcium signaling pathway and tyrosine metabolism while the deregulated genes were concentrated on cell cycle and endocytosis. As for the group by 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment, the significant pathways discerned by upregulated genes contained tyrosine metabolism GABAergic synapse and Hippo signaling pathway and downregulated genes were concentrated on cellular senescence, dopaminergic synapse and p53 signaling pathway, as was listed in Supplementary Tables 11–12. From the comparison of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group in Supplementary Tables 13–14, upregulated genes were enriched in ubiquinone as well as other terpenoid-quinone biosynthesis while downregulated genes were concentrated in renin-angiotensin system.

In the group of 25 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment, the remarkable pathways ascertained by upregulated genes included caffeine metabolism, Epstein-Barr virus infection and one carbon pool by folate signaling pathway. And the downregulated genes in the group treated by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs significantly enriched in Wnt signaling pathway, pathway in cancer and cell cycle, as summarized in Supplementary Tables 7–8. The changed pathways in the group exposed to 100 μg/mL N-GQDs were listed in Supplementary Tables 9–10. It was found that the upregulated genes were mainly enriched in calcium signaling pathway and tyrosine metabolism while the deregulated genes were concentrated on cell cycle and endocytosis. As for the group by 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment, the significant pathways discerned by upregulated genes contained tyrosine metabolism GABAergic synapse and Hippo signaling pathway and downregulated genes were concentrated on cellular senescence, dopaminergic synapse and p53 signaling pathway, as was listed in Supplementary Tables 11–12. From the comparison of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs group with 100 μg/mL A-GQDs group in Supplementary Tables 13–14, upregulated genes were enriched in ubiquinone as well as other terpenoid-quinone biosynthesis while downregulated genes were concentrated in renin-angiotensin system.

Based on the KEGG analysis (Fig. 3A–H), it could be found that (Cacna1d, Cacna1h and Ryr3) was related to the calcium signaling pathway, (Lef1 and Apc) participated in the Wnt signaling pathway, (Atm and Ccne2) acted an important part in DNA-damage and cell cycle, while Gad2 was associated with the GABAergic synapse. Therefore, these 8 genes were selected for the qRT-PCR verification, including 5 upregulated genes (Cacna1d, Cacna1h, Ryr3, Lef1, Gad2) and 3 downregulated genes (Atm, Ccne2, Apc). Furthermore, another 2 genes (Igf1, Mapk12) were also randomly chosen to participate in the verification to increase the reliability. From the results illustrated in Fig. 4A, 8 out of the 10 genes showed the same trend as the results from the microarray although the fold changes were not exactly the same, including 5 upregulated genes and 3 downregulated genes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein expression validation by Western blot
ce:section.ce:para: According to the KEGG results (Fig. 3A–H), it was found that the upregulated genes were mainly enriched in calcium signaling pathway in 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment group. Therefore, we used Western blot analysis to evaluate the expression of proteins that were either key components of Ca2+ signaling or important regulators of intracellular Ca2+ balance, including CACNA1D and RYR2. The results suggested that the expression of CACNA1D and RYR2 was increased by N-GQDs (Fig. 4B). As for the 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment group, the upregulated genes were mainly enriched in Hippo signaling pathway. And the protein expression level of p-YAP1 and the p-TAZ activity was decreased in BV2 cells after treated by 25 and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs (Fig. 4C). It was also found that the MAPK signaling pathway was a shared pathway in BV2 cells treated by 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs. JNK and p38 MAPK signaling pathways played important roles in cell cycle arrest and cell growth suppression, thus the protein expression levels of JNK/p-JNK and p38/p-p38 in BV2 cells was detected after exposure to 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs. After treatment of 24 h, the protein expression level of p-p38 was increased, while the p-JNK activity was decreased in BV2 cells, as shown in Fig. 4D.

The analysis of gene transduction was performed to identify the interaction between altered genes related to neurotoxicity by GQDs. And the network of genes or proteins interaction is regarded as a useful technique to deeply research the latent molecular mechanisms of xenobiotics. In our research, the signal-net analysis based on 1341 changed genes of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treated group as well as 1396 changed genes of 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treated group was carried out.

For the groups exposed to 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, 34 and 37 key genes were respectively gathered in the network analysis (Fig. 5A–D). According to the signaling pathway consequences, Gng8, Adcy4 and Cacna1d were involved in GABAergic synapse and calcium signaling pathway while Igf2, Insr, Atm, Rab11fip2 and Rab11b were related to Ras signaling pathway, DNA-damage as well as endocytosis for the group treated by 100 μg/mL N-GQDs. As for the group exposed to 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, Igf1, Epha2, Gli2 and Bmp4 were associated to Ras signaling pathway and Pathways in cancer, while Rspo1, Znrf3 and Rspo4 were relevant to Wnt signaling pathway. Thus it was reasonable to conclude that GABAergic synapse, calcium signaling pathway, Ras signaling pathway, DNA-damage as well as Wnt signaling pathway were likely to be the primary pathways resulting in the neurotoxicity by GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA damage and intracellular calcium overload by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The potential DNA damage caused by GQDs was evaluated by relevant indexes, including tail DNA%, tail length and olive tail moment. The BV2 cells treated with N-GQDs and A-GQDs were respectively compared with the cells of control group and the comet assay results were presented in Fig. 6A–D. It could be observed that the tail length in BV2 cells treated by N-GQDs and A-GQDs (25.66 ± 4.60 μm, 23.26 ± 4.78 μm, n = 50) for 24 h was longer than that of the control group (3.38 ± 1.20 μm, n = 50). Furthermore, the BV2 cells showed high sensibility to the DNA damage by GQDs and 24 h of exposure could result in significant increase in all three indexes. Compared to the A-GQDs treated group (tail DNA%: 17.93 ± 4.09; tail length: 23.26 ± 4.78 μm; olive tail moment: 5.08 ± 1.39), the DNA damage induced by N-GQDs (tail DNA%: 24.88 ± 3.81; tail length: 25.66 ± 4.60 μm; olive tail moment: 5.53 ± 1.77) was more serious.

Calcium signaling pathway contributed much to the cell damage induced by GQDs based on the KEGG pathway analysis, thus the calcium levels measurements were carried out in our research. After exposed to GQDs for 24 h, a distinct increase in the amount of intracellular calcium was observed in the BV2 cells, as shown in Fig. 7A. Interestingly, BAPTA-AM as intracellular Ca2+ chelator could eliminate the decreased cell viability by GQDs (Fig. 7B–C), further confirming the fact that the increased intracellular calcium concentration was related to BV2 cell damage and neurotoxicity induced by GQDs.

Worldwide researchers are paying much attention to monolayer and multilayer graphene because of their high-thermal conductivity, superior mechanical, electrical and optical properties as well as the band gaps with tunneling ability in the past few years (Ohta et al., 2006; Singh et al., 2011; Fitzer et al., 1995). And carbon nanostructures have exhibited various peculiarities, which can serve as the carrier for delivering the drugs to cells (Pei et al., 2020). As a typical carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, GQDs usually show excellent crystallinity and various advantages compared with carbon dots owning to the content of less crystalline sp2 carbon over carbon dots (Li et al., 2019). Therefore, GQDs have received increasing attention in many research areas, especially in the researches on biomedicine (Tetsuka et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a). In recent years, varieties of chemical functional GQDs are constantly developed by researchers, of which N-GQDs becomes a promising GQDs in environmental and biological applications because N-doping enhances the photochemical activity of GQDs for clear images with a high signal to noise ratio (Kaur et al., 2018). According to available studies, amino group functionalization is capable of promoting the linkage between graphene materials and organic molecules, which promises graphene-based nanoparticles more suitable for biomedical applications (Junkermeier et al., 2013; Sahiner et al., 2019). As fluorescent nanoprobes, N-GQDs and A-GQDs could contribute to the development of nano-diagnostics in the field of neuroscience due to their excellent optical properties, while they should be evaluated before application in living organisms. Therefore, it deserves great concern to explore the connection between the phenotypic toxicity and internal mechanisms by use of advanced techniques. In this research, the transcriptomic analysis was carried out to thoroughly explore the related genes as well as underlying pathways participating in the neurotoxicity of BV2 cell by GQDs.

The bioinformatics analysis was applied for the toxic mechanism research following microarray detection, including screening of diversely expressed genes, GO annotation analysis as well as KEGG pathway research. Furthermore, several pathways by which GQDs led to neurotoxicity was supposed for further confirmation although it was not being completely revealed. After treated for 24 h by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs, the upregulated expression of voltage-gated ion channel activity genes appeared, demonstrating that the voltage-gated ion channel could be acutely activated in a relatively low concentration. As for the groups exposed to 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, the neuronal action potential imbalance resulting from redox reaction and active voltage-gated ion channels was possibly the main factor responsible for the impairment of BV2 cells. Totally speaking, downregulated genes were partially included in cell cycle response and endocytosis in the BV2 cells treated by relatively high concentrations of GQDs. Furthermore, some classifications in the biological processes were extensively down-regulated, containing the activities of sulfuric ester hydrolase and arylsulfatase.

The underlying biological processes related to the bad effects of GQDs were achieved by GO, KEGG pathway and signal-net analysis. In the experimental group treated by 25 μg/mL N-GQDs, there appeared distinguished changes of several genes. The upregulated genes were remarkably enriched in a carbon sink by folate. And the downregulated genes were obviously gathered in Wnt signaling pathway as well as pathways in cancer and cell cycle. Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2020b) found that graphene oxide treatment dysregulated Frizzled receptor MOM-5, Disheveled protein DSH-2, GSK-3 (a component of APC complex) and two β-catenin proteins (BAR-1 and HMP-2). This would damage the functional state of intestinal barrier in the intestine of Caenorhabditis elegans, which was interestingly consistent with our research to some extent. As for the 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment group, the upregulated genes were distinctly enriched in the pathways of GABAergic synapse, calcium signaling pathway and tyrosine metabolism, which were inferentially related to the neurotoxicity by GQDs. Furthermore, a bunch of downregulated genes was concentrated on cell cycle, endocytosis and cellular senescence in this group. It could be explained that the downregulation of ATM and the closely related kinase ATR mRNA led to DNA damage, cell cycle arrest and cell viability decrease, which were considered as dominate controllers of cell cycle checkpoint signaling pathways essential for genome stability as well as cell response to DNA damage. From the KEGG pathway analysis of 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment group, the upregulated genes were extensively gathered in the pathways of tyrosine metabolism and GABAergic synapse, while a bunch of downregulated genes were concentrated on cellular senescence and p53 signaling pathway.

Based on the microarray consequences, calcium channel activity and cell cycle were respectively the consistent GO terms and KEGG pathway that mRNA gathered in BV2 cells exposed to different concentrations of N-GQDs (25 and 100 μg/mL). This suggested that the calcium channel activity and cell cycle were related to the cellular bad effects of N-GQDs from non-toxic dose to slight toxic dose. Meanwhile, the Wnt signaling pathway in 25 μg/mL N-GQDs exposure group was also vital since they were likely to participate in the early bioprocess without evident cytotoxicity and genotoxicity. Surprisingly, the endocytosis was downregulated in BV2 cells treated by GQDs, which could be interpreted using the compensation mechanism. The extensive decrease of endocytosis was possibly attributed to the inverse feedback from preventative measures, thus triggering the compensation mechanisms to take defensive measures for releasing the toxicity. Therefore, it was reasonable to predict that the downregulation of endocytosis pathway maintained the cellular homeostasis by inverse feedback regulation. The tyrosine metabolism and GABAergic synapse were the consistent KEGG signaling pathways in both groups exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs. GABA transaminase with GABA (A) and GABA (B) receptors was considered to be a neurochemical biomarker and the abundant unsaturated fatty acids made the brain sensitive to dysregulation of lipid metabolism (Legradi et al., 2018). In general, GABAergic synapse, calcium signaling pathway, tyrosine metabolism and cell cycle might be the toxic pathways by GQDs. Furthermore, the differences in the toxic pathways from GQDs with diversely chemical functionalization were explored by analyzing the mRNAs in 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and 100 μg/mL A-GQDs treatment groups. Compared with the group treated by 100 μg/mL A-GQDs, a bunch of upregulated genes in 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment group were interestingly found to be enriched in Ubiquinone and other terpenoid-quinone biosynthesis. It was worth noting that Ubiquinone was the carrier of hydrogen in the respiratory chain. Thus the alteration of Ubiquinone would unavoidably affect the biological processes such as mitochondrial electron transport, leading to the abnormal metabolism in vivo. Therefore, it was reasonable that the lower cell survival rate of 100 μg/mL N-GQDs treatment group than 100 μg/mL A-GQDs exposure group might be originated from the maladjustment of Ubiquinone pathway.

Several research groups have investigated the toxicity of GQDs and reported that the toxic effect of GQDs mainly included immune, inflammation and cell biology processes (Li et al., 2018; Sasidharan et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2017). In spite of the different GQDs used in our research, the underlying toxic mechanisms were comparable to some extent. Some typical signaling pathways were interconnected with immune and pro-inflammatory reaction responding to GQDs, including calcium signaling pathway, MAPK signaling pathway and Wnt signaling pathway. Interestingly, several primary genes or proteins related to these typical pathways were observed to be extensively changed in this study in spite of different cell lines and GQDs used for assessment. JNK and p38 MAPK signaling pathways served as critical roles in cell cycle arrest and growth suppression. In this study, the protein expression level of p-p38 was elevated while the p-JNK activity was reduced in BV2 cells treated by 100 μg/mL N-GQDs and A-GQDs, indicating the suppressed cell cycle. A previous research reported that OH-GQDs could suppress the cell cycle process of human esophageal epithelial cells (HET-1A) and facilitate H2AX phosphorylation in a dose-dependent manner, thus leading to DNA damage as well as aggravating genomic instability in HET-1A cells (Li et al., 2018). Similarly, the down-regulation of ATM mRNA was detected in our present study and the single cell gel electrophoresis consequence confirmed the DNA damage, which would activate the DNA damage response of cells. And the cell cycle process was interdicted to provide enough time for repairing DNA damage. Furthermore, the cell death process would be launched if the damage was irreparable. Of note, the triggering dose (thresholds) varied between different cell lines treated by diverse GQDs containing various components although the toxic mechanisms seemed to be similar. And the assessment of cytotoxicity as well as latent damage target is unmeaning expect that it is under the practical treated level and treatment route (Wang and Tang, 2018).

qRT-PCR verification was carried out to validate the microarray consequences on account of the possible false positive and technological error. In this study, 8 genes showing the same trend from two experimental groups were selected to take in the validation. And the tendencies from qRT-PCR agreed well with the microarray consequences, indicating the reliability of microarray analysis. As well, it demonstrated that the neurotoxicology of GQDs was possibly mediated by these extensively changed genes as well as relevant pathways. It was worth noting that the essential difference between various cell lines should be noticed.

Ca2+ signaling as a second messenger acts a vital part in both physiological and pathological functions. And the excess intracellular calcium amount contributes much to the cytotoxic effects, which will result in extensive physiological disorder including ER function damage, chromatin injury and even cell death (Deweirdt et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2015). The ER provides the major site of protein modification by amino-linked glycosylation, disulfide bonds formation and subsequent folding. A published study reported that aberrant Ca2+ homeostasis would lead to unnecessary ER pressure, which might be related to multiple cell death modes, (e.g. pyroptosis, apoptosis, autophagy) (Wang et al., 2020). Furthermore, graphene oxide would destroy the lipid composition, Ca2+ homeostasis as well as synaptic transmission in primary cortical neurons (Bramini et al., 2016). As well, some researchers reported that the elevated intracellular Ca2+ concentration [Ca2+]i would result in JNK phosphorylation and cause autophagy through LC3 (Lim et al., 2016). From the KEGG analysis, the calcium signaling pathway served as a key role in the neurotoxicity of BV2 cells by GQDs. Therefore, [Ca2+]i was measured to validate the consequence. It was found that intracellular Ca2+ was raised in a dose-dependent manner. And the Ca2+ that induced the [Ca2+]i signal mainly originated from two sources: extracellular Ca2+ passing through the plasma membrane and intracellular Ca2+ storage. Furthermore, the upregulation of CaV1 and CaV3 genes was a main factor responsible for the elevated [Ca2+]i in that voltage-gated Ca2+-selective channels (CaVs) were the fastest Ca2+ signaling proteins, which would cause drastic alteration within a single cell. As well, the upregulated ryanodine receptors (RyR) suggested the released Ca2+ from ER.

The toxicity of GQDs is attracting widespread attention considering their extensive application prospect as well as their increasing exposure to the public and environment. It is crucial to search feasible preventive biomarker as well as specific pathway in order to protect the susceptible population. In this research, the pathways responsible for the neurotoxicity by GQDs (different chemical functionalization or concentrations) were investigated. From the whole analysis above, calcium signaling pathway, DNA damage, cell cycle arrest and metabolic activation were closely associated with the induced neurotoxicity by GQDs. Therefore, a schematic diagram for the underlying mechanisms involved in GQDs induced BV2 cell damage was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . Considering the possible effects of these overlapping signaling pathways on GQDs-induced neurotoxicity, it is meaningful to concern these consistent signaling pathways. In addition, the dysregulation of many molecules constantly serves a main role in the regulation of cellular processes from early cellular adverse effects to eventual cell damage. Thus it was responsible to conclude that complex signaling networks could disclose the underlying toxic mechanisms of GQDs to a large extent.
